source_files: Elicit - Gut Microbiome and Health A Bidirectional Review - Report.pdf

findings.pattern: Reduced overall diversity, depleted beneficial Lachnospiraceae and Ruminococcaceae families, expansion of Proteobacteria including pathogenic Enterobacteriaceae. UC and Crohn's show consistent reductions in Faecalibacterium prausnitzii, a keystone butyrate producer with established anti-inflammatory properties. Adherent invasive Escherichia coli colonization associated specifically with ileal Crohn's disease.
findings.mechanism: Intestinal dysbiosis coupled with reduced SCFA-producing capacity and barrier dysfunction
findings.caution: Many associations from cross-sectional studies; correlation vs causation not established

findings.pattern: Lower alpha diversity and enrichment of Gram-negative bacteria compared to healthy controls. Shiella species associated significantly with IBS; Bifidobacterium rectale and Faecalibacterium prausnitzii reduced in some populations. Dysphysiological pathophysiology suggested altered gut-brain signaling.
findings.mechanism: Dysregulation of gut-brain axis via altered microbial metabolite production; increased intestinal motility perturbations, permeability, visceral hypersensitivity
findings.caution: Whether microbiome changes represent causative factors or consequences of underlying pathophysiology remains incompletely resolved

findings.pattern: Increased Fusobacterium, Lactobacillus reuteri, Bacteroides fragilis, and Staphylococcus aureus with lower Methanobrevibacter, Lachnospira plantarum, and Akkermansia muciphila. Distinct dysbiotic signature from other metabolic diseases.
findings.mechanism: Altered metabolic capacity and energy harvest mechanisms; reduced SCFA production
findings.study_type: Comparative dysbiosis analysis across metabolic disorders
findings.caution: ~50% overlap in microbiome changes across metabolic diseases; distinct signatures not absolute

findings.pattern: Higher Fusobacterium and Ruminococcus albus, elevated Clostridium clusters, lower Akkermansia muciphila, Faecalibacterium, Roseburia, and Bacteroides vulgatus. Several Bifidobacterium species reduced. More granular analysis identifies increased Proteobacteria phylum, Enterobacteriaceae family, and Escherichia.
findings.mechanism: Reduced SCFA production; increased endotoxemia (LPS) from Gram-negative expansion; impaired glucose homeostasis signaling
findings.study_type: Metabolic profiling and comparative dysbiosis studies
findings.caution: Cross-sectional overlap with obesity and NAFLD dysbiosis (~50%); causality unclear

findings.pattern: Lower Firmicutes, Rikenellaceae, and Ruminococcaceae families with elevated Escherichia and Lactobacillus. More granular analysis identified increased Proteobacteria phylum, Enterobacteriaceae family, and Escherichia species, with elevated genera Rikenellaceae, Ruminococcaceae, Faecalibacterium, Coprococcus, Anaerostipes, and Eubacterium.
findings.mechanism: Altered lipid metabolism signaling; increased bacterial lipopolysaccharide (LPS) translocation; reduced short-chain fatty acid production
findings.caution: Distinct yet overlapping with obesity and T2D dysbiosis; ~50% microbiome overlap across metabolic disorders

findings.disease: Autoimmune Conditions (SLE, RA, MS, T1D, Psoriasis)
findings.pattern: Shared common dysbiotic features: reduction in microbial diversity, increased intestinal permeability, expansion of pro-inflammatory species like Ruminococcus gnavus. Systemic analysis across lupus erythematosus, rheumatoid arthritis, multiple sclerosis, type 1 diabetes, and psoriasis revealed reduced microbial diversity, increased intestinal permeability, and expansion of pro-inflammatory species. Specific dysbiosis patterns connected with autoimmune uveitis, ocular Sjögren's syndrome, and dermatological manifestations of immune dysregulation.
findings.mechanism: Pro-inflammatory species expansion drives IL-6, IL-10, and TNF-alpha production; impaired regulatory T cell (Treg) and TH17 differentiation; molecular mimicry mechanisms
findings.study_type: Systematic cross-disease analysis and mechanistic studies
findings.caution: ~50% microbiome overlap across autoimmune diseases; many associations from cross-sectional studies

findings.pattern: Increased abundance of Prevotella and Clostridium with lower levels of Alistipes finegoldii and Lactobacillus. Heart failure characterized by reduced alpha diversity correlated with elevated inflammatory biomarkers and endotoxemia (lipopolysaccharide levels). Higher Shannon diversity index values associated with lower inflammation and endotoxemia.
findings.mechanism: Increased bacterial translocation of lipopolysaccharide (LPS); enhanced systemic endotoxemia and inflammatory responses; impaired short-chain fatty acid production; altered trimethylamine (TMA) and trimethylamine N-oxide (TMAO) metabolism
findings.study_type: Cross-sectional diversity and mechanistic studies; advanced intervention trials
findings.caution: Correlation between microbiota diversity and inflammatory markers; causality remains to be established in intervention studies

findings.pattern: Approximately 50% overlap in microbiome alterations across distinct disease categories. Common features include: reduced alpha diversity; decreased beneficial Lachnospiraceae, Ruminococcaceae, Faecalibacterium; increased Proteobacteria and pathogenic Gram-negatives (Enterobacteriaceae, Escherichia); elevated LPS-producing bacteria; reduced SCFA-producing capacity.
findings.mechanism: Common pathways: barrier dysfunction via reduced butyrate; increased bacterial lipopolysaccharide translocation and endotoxemia; dysregulated immune tolerance mechanisms; altered tryptophan metabolite (indole derivative) signaling
findings.caution: High heterogeneity across studies; distinct dysbiotic signatures reflect disease-specific immune responses overlaid on common dysbiotic foundation

findings.pattern: Antibiotic exposure induces substantial perturbations: reduced diversity (β = -0.87, P < 0.05), increased Streptococcus-dominated states (β = 1.83, P < 0.05) and meropenem transitions to Enterococcus-dominated communities (β = 3.69, P < 0.05). Metformin alters composition in T2D patients. Proton pump inhibitors disrupt diversity and increase Clostridium difficile risk. Vitamin D supplementation increases anti-inflammatory bacteria including Bifidobacterium and Faecalibacterium, with elevated Bacteroides-to-Firmicutes ratio.
findings.mechanism: Selective pressure on bacterial communities; altered pharmacokinetics and microbiome-mediated drug metabolism; host immune modulation
findings.caution: Medication effects often confound disease-microbiome associations in cross-sectional studies; distinguishing medication vs disease effects challenging

study_design_cautions: Approximately 50% overlap in microbiome alterations across distinct disease categories limits disease-specific diagnostic value
Many associations derived from cross-sectional studies; correlation does not establish causation
Heterogeneous intervention trial designs and meta-analysis limitations prevent robust comparative efficacy conclusions
methodological_limitations: Sample size requirements for adequately powered microbiome studies exceed most published investigations
Taxonomic profiling inadequately captures microbiome metabolic activity; bacteria detected via DNA sequencing may be dead, dormant, or metabolically inactive
Low-abundance species difficult to detect due to sequencing depth limitations; these organisms could exert disproportionate ecosystem influence
Food composition databases lack comprehensive data on resistant starch, oligosaccharide content, and polyphenol profiles
data_quality_notes.interpretation_guidance: Dysbiotic signatures reflect disease-specific immune responses overlaid on common dysbiotic foundation. Shared pathways include barrier dysfunction via reduced butyrate production, increased bacterial lipopolysaccharide (LPS) translocation, dysregulated immune tolerance, and altered tryptophan metabolite signaling. Distinct dysbiotic signatures do not represent disease-specific biomarkers but rather disease-specific inflammatory responses to common dysbiotic patterns.
